node.data: The article discusses the classification of conditional sentences within the context of linguistics, focusing on their grammatical structure, semantics, and implications for natural language processing and automated reasoning. It explores how conditional logic and propositional logic can be applied to enhance understanding and classification of these sentences in communication.
G1

This article discusses techniques for improving the recognition of hand-printed text by utilizing context analysis to correct classification errors, building on previous work related to scanning and preprocessing characters. It is part of a broader research initiative focused on enhancing text recognition through various interdisciplinary approaches, including cognitive science and computer vision.
G2

The article "Syntactic Analysis of English By Computer - A Survey" explores the complexities of spoken language communication, emphasizing the limitations of linear string representations and the need for sophisticated processes to decode and understand the intended meaning. It discusses the interplay between encoding and decoding in communication, highlighting the relevance of syntactic and semantic analysis in computational linguistics and natural language processing.
G3

L'article discute du théorème de Weierstrass sur la représentation des fonctions continues par des séries uniformément convergentes, soulignant son importance dans la théorie analytique des fonctions et son application dans le développement de la théorie des fonctions génératrices d'Abel. Il met en lumière les avancées théoriques apportées par Weierstrass, tout en notant les limites pratiques de sa méthode.
G4

The article by Dr. Loring discusses methods for studying controlled word associations, presenting a comprehensive list of 10,888 experimentally selected words to facilitate psychobiological investigations. It highlights the historical applications of these methods in diagnosing psychological conditions and detecting guilty knowledge, emphasizing their significance in both research and practical settings.
G5

The article presents a comprehensive framework of postulates for the science of language, encompassing various subfields such as applied and theoretical linguistics, cognitive linguistics, and natural language processing, while addressing key concepts like syntax, semantics, and language assessment. It aims to establish foundational principles that guide the study and understanding of language in its diverse forms and applications.
G6

The article discusses pure word-deafness, a rare speech disorder characterized by the inability to understand spoken language while retaining the ability to speak, read, and write. It reviews historical observations and theories regarding the condition, including potential causes and diagnostic methods, highlighting its neglect in recent research despite its significance in the study of language disorders.
G7

The article critiques the practice of compiling slang from specific communities, such as soldiers and jazz musicians, highlighting how many terms are often invented or lack genuine usage among the intended groups. It argues that these compilations can misrepresent the language and culture of these communities, as they frequently rely on superficial or outdated expressions rather than authentic, commonly used vernacular.
G8

The article by A. J. P. Martin discusses various methodologies and techniques in summarizing texts, focusing on fields such as linguistics, natural language processing, and information visualization, while exploring applications in journalism and communication. It emphasizes the importance of automatic summarization and multi-modal approaches in enhancing document analysis and narrative comprehension.
G9

The article presents a novel approach to estimating the entropy and redundancy of the English language by utilizing statistical knowledge from native speakers and experimental data on predicting the next letter based on preceding text. It includes experimental results and explores the characteristics of an ideal predictor in this context.

The article discusses a speech recognition system capable of accurately identifying spoken digits at normal speech rates, achieving an accuracy of 97 to 99 percent for individual speakers. It details the system's methodology, which involves analyzing frequency bands and utilizing a two-dimensional portrayal for recognition, although it notes limitations in performance with multiple speakers without adjustments.

The article "Die Ziqpu-Gestirne nach neuen Keilschrifttexten" explores the Ziqpu constellations based on newly discovered cuneiform texts, contributing to the understanding of ancient astronomical knowledge in Mesopotamia. Published in the Zeitschrift für Assyriologie und Vorderasiatische Archäologie, it highlights the significance of these texts in the study of ancient Near Eastern cultures.

The article explores various linguistic challenges associated with machine translation, highlighting both previously unaddressed issues and reexamining those that have been discussed in the past from a new perspective. It aims to contribute to the methodological understanding of these problems within the field of natural language processing.

The article discusses predictive coding, a method for transmitting messages by encoding the difference between actual and predicted values, emphasizing its applications in various fields such as computer science, machine learning, and cognitive science. It outlines the foundational concepts of predictive coding and sets the stage for further exploration of its mathematical criteria and efficient coding examples in the subsequent part.

The article examines how the frequency of word usage influences the clustering phenomenon in the recall of randomly arranged words, contributing to the fields of linguistics, text mining, and machine learning by exploring patterns in memory and language processing.

The article explores various models of linguistic structure to assess their effectiveness in generating English sentences, concluding that while finite-state Markov processes are inadequate, more complex phrase structure grammars and transformational approaches offer a more powerful and insightful method for understanding language. It highlights the formal properties of grammatical transformations and their implications for language description and processing.

The article explores the extension of Marbe's Law, which describes the inverse relationship between reaction times and the frequency of associated responses, to the recall of stimulus words. Through a study involving undergraduate students, it investigates how the rank of words in a stimulus list affects recall and reaction times, emphasizing the influence of habit strength and social background on cognitive processing.

The article discusses a statistical approach to improving the mechanized encoding and searching of literary information, highlighting the challenges of conveying meaning through written communication and the variability in individual expression. It outlines a systematic method that includes document analysis, vocabulary establishment, and the use of automated encoding and search techniques to enhance information retrieval in literature.

The article discusses the techniques and importance of note-taking in consecutive interpretation, highlighting its role in enhancing comprehension and retention of information during the interpretation process. It explores various strategies and syntactic structures that can aid interpreters in effectively capturing and conveying messages.

This article discusses the properties of absolutely convergent Dirichlet series and their relationship with semigroups of positive integers, presenting an affirmative answer to a specific mathematical query regarding the form of certain functions. It also explores the structure of commutative Banach algebras formed from these series and their implications in the context of l1-algebras and Fourier transforms.

The article presents studies on word associations in French to test the generalizability of findings from English-language experiments, revealing similarities in associative responses between the two languages and confirming three out of four hypotheses regarding recall influences in associative grouping tasks.

The article "Perceptual Recognition of Words" by Leo Postman and Mark R. Rosenzweig explores the cognitive processes involved in recognizing and understanding words, focusing on aspects of speech perception and language processing. It discusses the implications of these processes for fields such as psycholinguistics and natural language processing.

The article "Prediction Of Missing Words In Sentences" explores methods for predicting absent words in sentences, focusing on the implications for language comprehension and processing within the fields of applied linguistics and cognitive science. It discusses theoretical considerations and research findings relevant to understanding how individuals fill in gaps in language, contributing to advancements in natural language processing and reading research.

The article presents an algorithm designed to analyze logical statements and generate corresponding truth function tables, contributing to the fields of knowledge representation, reasoning, and computational logic within artificial intelligence and computer science.

The article discusses an experiment that investigates the impact of different parts of a printed word on readers' ability to recognize the whole word, revealing that alterations at the beginning of a word are more disruptive to recognition than changes made in the middle or at the end.

The article explores associative clustering in the recall of words from lists categorized by different taxonomic frequencies, detailing a methodology for measuring the incidence of word clusters during free recall tasks. It highlights the significance of word frequency on recall performance and the implications for fields such as linguistics and data science.

This article explores the potential of interlingual machine translation, emphasizing the use of a logically formalized interlingua to facilitate translation between any two languages through a structured network of semantic elements and dictionaries. It discusses the methodology for constructing a comprehensive array of symbols and the process of deriving translations by systematic comparisons.

The article discusses a fundamental method of speech synthesis that involves joining discrete segments of recorded utterances to create continuous speech, focusing on the influence of phonetic elements and the importance of stable positions in articulation. It also highlights the various techniques required for effective segmentation, while noting the limitations related to dialect and speaker variability.

The article compares two methods of obtaining normative free association data: discrete single word responses and continued association, highlighting the differences in response patterns and the implications for understanding word meaningfulness and associations. It discusses the need for further empirical validation of these methods and presents findings from a study analyzing associations from various participants.

The article discusses the development of a computer program designed to recognize ten English vowels in isolated words, achieving an overall accuracy of 88% across 21 subjects, which increased to 93% when considering vowel duration. The program utilizes real-time spectral data and various techniques to refine the recognition of vowel formants.

The article details the design and functionality of a mechanical speech recognizer developed at University College London, highlighting its key components such as the acoustic spectrum analyzer and phoneme sequence memory, as well as discussing the results achieved and potential future applications in speech technology and assistive devices.

The article "The Ordinary Language Tree" by Fred Sommers explores the structure and characteristics of ordinary language through the lens of linguistics and computational linguistics, focusing on the concept of a language tree to analyze natural language processing. Published in the journal Mind, it delves into the complexities of language and its representation in a tree-like format.

The article discusses the Cauchy problem for hyperbolic systems, highlighting classical results and various methods proposed for simplification, particularly through the lens of semigroup theory. It emphasizes the use of singular integral operators to address technical difficulties in the problem, while noting specific limitations and referencing foundational works in the field.

The article discusses the development and analysis of MAUDE (Morse Automatic Decoder), a specialized digital computer designed to decode hand-sent Morse code messages with a high accuracy rate of 90-95% across 184 operators, making it a practical tool for environments with heavy communication traffic. It highlights MAUDE's capabilities in recognizing Morse code patterns and its potential for improvement through the integration of more sophisticated language rules.

The article discusses the challenges of accurately measuring premorbid intelligence to evaluate intellectual deterioration, emphasizing the need for valid measures that remain unaffected by cognitive decline. It highlights the methodological difficulties in establishing a baseline for cognitive function before and after the onset of disorders, while also touching on related fields such as cognitive science and language processing.

This article explores the constraints imposed by the decodability condition on code word sets, introducing a generating function that relates to "full" words and discusses the concept of "completable" strings in the context of various fields such as software design, coding theory, and natural language processing.

The article explores the retention of connected discourse in comparison to traditional studies using nonsense syllables and unconnected words, highlighting the lack of research in this area and proposing a new scoring system to measure recall of meaningful prose. It examines whether the same governing variables apply to both connected and unconnected materials, aiming to enhance understanding of how meaningful language is retained.

The article "A Study of Clustering in Free Recall Based on Synonyms" explores how synonyms influence the clustering of information during free recall tasks, contributing to the understanding of semantic similarity and its implications for fields such as text mining and natural language processing. It examines the cognitive processes involved in memory retrieval and clustering, providing insights relevant to machine learning and data science.

"Studies in Words" explores the intricate relationship between language and meaning through various themes such as nature, emotion, and consciousness, while also examining the implications of these concepts in fields like linguistics, applied linguistics, and literary studies. The article serves as a comprehensive guide to understanding the nuances of language and its impact on communication and thought.

The article "Symbol Manipulation by Threaded Lists" by A. J. Perlis and Charles Thornton explores the use of threaded lists for efficient symbol manipulation in computer science, with implications for natural language processing and human-computer interaction. It discusses the theoretical foundations and practical applications of this approach, contributing to advancements in symbolic computation and graph theory.

The article discusses the characteristics of constructive models in mathematics, focusing on predicates with multiple places and the implications for logical formulas and realizations within these models. It explores the intersection of constructive logic with various fields, including natural language processing and applied mathematics.

The article "Sense Without Denotation" by Timothy Smiley explores the relationship between meaning and reference in language, focusing on how semantic interpretation can occur without direct denotation. It discusses implications for fields such as linguistics, computer science, and natural language processing, particularly in the context of knowledge representation and reasoning.

The article "An Introduction to Information Processing Language V" by A. Newell and F. M. Tonge provides an overview of a programming language designed for information processing, discussing its applications in fields such as psychology, natural language processing, and information retrieval. Published in April 1960, it highlights the significance of this language in advancing computer science and information science.

The article discusses a study on spoken digit recognition from zero to nine using a digital computer simulation that employs time-frequency pattern matching for recognition. It highlights the effectiveness and limitations of the method, noting successful recognition rates for single speakers and challenges when including a female speaker, while emphasizing the potential for improving performance through time normalization and pattern matching techniques.

The article "The Quick Word Test" published in the Journal of Educational Research discusses a language assessment tool designed to evaluate vocabulary and language proficiency, highlighting its relevance in the context of natural language processing and educational testing methodologies.

The article discusses an assembly program designed for a phrase structure language that enables users to define the meanings of both procedural and declarative statements, offering a more flexible approach than existing macro compilation systems. It was presented at the Harrogate Conference of the British Computer Society on July 5, 1960, and touches on themes of computational semantics and natural language processing.

The article "Sense Without Denotation" by Timothy Smiley explores the concepts of word-sense disambiguation and lexical semantics, focusing on the relationship between meaning and reference in natural language processing and formal semantics. It discusses the implications of these ideas for knowledge representation, reasoning, and automated deduction in the fields of linguistics and computer science.

The article examines the process of abstract formation through sentence selection, comparing the effectiveness of human and machine methods. It highlights that while human selections show less variability than random chance, they still differ significantly from machine-generated selections, indicating a lack of consensus between the two approaches.

The article investigates how various stimulus parameters influence word-recognition thresholds, revealing that factors such as word classification, absence of prefixes, and logarithmic word frequencies significantly affect recognition. The findings suggest that familiarity with concrete nouns enhances recognition, indicating a strong relationship between prior experiences and language processing.

The article discusses the advancements in machine recognition of speech, highlighting its foundations in electrical engineering and its connections to various fields such as computer science, linguistics, and machine learning. It reflects on the development of technologies related to speech processing and natural language understanding, emphasizing their significance in communication and language recognition.

The article discusses the recognition scene in Aeschylus's "Choephori," arguing for the authenticity of certain verses that were previously dismissed, and explores the implications of Aristotle's attribution regarding physical similarity. It also examines the challenges of visualizing the original production and offers insights into the realism in the works of Aeschylus and Euripides.

The article discusses the development of a table look-up machine designed for natural language processing, which utilizes a photographic memory for efficient retrieval of lexical information and supports grammatical contextual analysis through innovative addressing features. This machine aims to enhance language recognition and processing capabilities by enabling operations based on linguistic determinations from retrieved data.

The article discusses a study by G. E. Peterson and H. L. Barney on the relationship between vowel sounds in English and their corresponding formant frequencies, highlighting the challenges of computer interpretation due to complex boundaries in the data. It presents a transformation method that simplifies these boundaries for improved computer identification of vowel types.

The article explores the intersection of linguistics and applied linguistics, focusing on how language functions in communication through various aspects such as syntax, semantics, and language acquisition, while also addressing the implications for natural language processing and speech processing. It presents a series of lectures that delve into the theoretical and practical applications of language in understanding and facilitating effective communication.

The article explores two distinct linguistic codes linked to social structures, examining how these codes influence cognitive behavior and self-regulation. It analyzes speech samples from middle-class and working-class groups with varying IQ profiles to investigate the relationship between social class, linguistic usage, and hesitation phenomena in communication.

The article explores the conditioning of textual responses in young children using extrinsic reinforcers, demonstrating how different reinforcement conditions can influence the acquisition of new texts. The findings suggest that operant conditioning principles can be applied to understand and enhance human behavior in language learning and processing.

The article discusses methods for retrieving misspelled names within an airline's passenger record system, focusing on techniques related to error correction and information retrieval in the context of data science and natural language processing.

The article discusses a computer program designed for pitch extraction in speech processing, which combines three separate tests to achieve results that surpass individual methods. The program has shown promising outcomes in enhancing Vocoder quality, indicating its potential for improving speech recognition and synthesis applications.

The article investigates the neural mechanisms behind letter recognition in words presented to the left and right of a fixation point, revealing that recognition is superior for letters on the right side. It discusses the symmetrical distribution of errors in letter positions and explores implications for fields such as linguistics, natural language processing, and character recognition.

The article discusses the Shadow language, which is utilized for syntactic analysis by digital computers, detailing a subroutine that processes input strings and produces a trace table of analysis results. It also summarizes previously unpublished reports and explores broader issues related to problem-oriented languages and transformations in the context of computational linguistics and natural language processing.

The article presents a revised report on the algorithmic language ALGOL 60, detailing its implementation and development within the field of computer science. Authored by notable figures in the discipline, it discusses the language's significance in programming and algorithm design, contributing to the theoretical foundations of computation.

The article discusses the development and application of the General Inquirer, an IBM 7090 program created at Harvard for content analysis in behavioral sciences, and its evolution into an automatic theme analysis tool through its merger with Hunt Concept Learner. It also explores the rationale behind its development and highlights recent advancements and future potential in the fields of pattern recognition, natural language processing, and information retrieval.

The article "A Computational Approach to Grammatical Coding of English Words" by Sheldon Klein and Robert F. Simmons explores methods for the computational analysis and grammatical coding of English words, contributing to the fields of computational linguistics and natural language processing. It discusses the implications of these methods for semantic interpretation and the broader understanding of the English language.

The article explores Miller's chunk hypothesis by examining the recall of categorized word lists, suggesting that the number of word categories recalled should match that of unrelated words. It discusses methodologies involving keyword extraction, topic modeling, and natural language processing to enhance automatic classification and information retrieval in the context of word recall.

The article discusses the challenges and advancements in developing error-correcting parse algorithms for compilers, highlighting the difficulties in handling syntactically incorrect code in programming languages like ALGOL and FORTRAN. It emphasizes the need for improved error detection and recovery methods to enhance the efficiency of compiler construction and the generation of optimized machine code.

The article "Quotients of Context-Free Languages" by Seymour Ginsburg and Edwin H. Spanier explores the properties and implications of quotients in context-free languages, contributing to the understanding of formal language theory and its applications in computational linguistics. Published in the ACM Journal, it discusses the mathematical foundations and significance of these language structures.

The article explores the phenomenon of semantic interference in color-naming tasks, where participants struggle to name the color of ink when the color word is incongruent, highlighting the cognitive challenges posed by the interaction between language and perception. It discusses the implications of this interference for understanding semantic power and its effects on linguistic processing.

The article explores the computer interpretation of English text and schematic pictures in scientific technical documents, proposing a unified syntactic analysis approach for both types of information. It discusses the application of artificial language techniques to enhance natural language processing and presents an algorithm for matching sentences to a simple grammar for improved interpretation.

The article "A Preliminary Functional Model for Language Behaviour" presents an initial framework for understanding language processing and behavior through the lens of psycholinguistics and behavioral sciences, exploring the cognitive mechanisms involved in natural language processing. It aims to contribute to the broader field of language science by proposing a model that integrates linguistic theory with insights into brain function.

This article presents a computer system that employs coordinate indexing and structure matching techniques to effectively extract criteria from English questions for answer selection. It details a process that includes indexing content words, analyzing dependencies for semantic evaluation, and utilizing a human editor to address syntactic ambiguities in question-answering systems.

The article analyzes reading errors in statistical approximations, categorizing them into omissions and substitutions, and explores the relationship between these errors and theories of immediate memory. It proposes a model linking "thought units" to Chomsky's grammar, addressing the limitations of information theory in fully capturing natural language sequences and offering insights into semantics and reading comprehension.

The article discusses a system designed for the automatic recognition of handwritten words, highlighting the challenges posed by the variability in handwriting compared to more standardized character recognition tasks. It emphasizes the importance of this technology in pattern recognition and its applications in fields such as linguistics, machine learning, and natural language processing.

The article "Correspondence between ALGOL 60 and Church's Lambda-notation" by P. J. Landin explores the relationship between the ALGOL 60 programming language and Church's Lambda-notation, highlighting their similarities and implications for computer science and programming language theory. It provides insights into how these two systems can be understood in relation to each other, contributing to the fields of linguistics and natural language processing.

The article "A New Normal-Form Theorem for Context-Free Phrase Structure Grammars" by Sheila A. Greibach presents a novel theorem that enhances the understanding of context-free grammars, contributing to the fields of linguistics and computational linguistics by providing a new framework for syntactic parsing and grammar structure.

The article explores the computational complexity of recognizing context-free and context-sensitive languages through the lens of memory bounds in multitape Turing machines, introducing a novel "translational" method that refines existing hierarchies and examines the translational properties of various language classes and related monotonic functions.

The article "Answering English Questions by Computer" by R. F. Simmons provides a comprehensive survey of the advancements in computational linguistics and natural language processing aimed at enabling computers to effectively answer questions posed in English. It discusses various technologies and methodologies related to speech recognition, language assessment, and computer-assisted language learning.

The article presents normative data for the Multiple Affect Adjective Check List (MAACL), detailing findings from various normal and psychiatric samples, including job applicants and college students, to highlight significant differences in affective responses among these groups. It emphasizes the importance of stratifying samples by demographic variables to ensure accurate representation in psychological assessments.

The article reports on two experiments examining how individuals search through word lists for targets defined by meaning, revealing that this semantic search process is significantly slower than searching for known words. It discusses the two stages of word processing—stimulus examination and memory retrieval—while also presenting preliminary data on error rates and other related factors.

The article discusses ELIZA, a pioneering computer program developed by Joseph Weizenbaum at MIT, designed to explore natural language communication between humans and machines, highlighting its significance in the fields of artificial intelligence and human-computer interaction.

This report explores the properties of context-free grammars, focusing on their structure, ambiguity, and relationship to finite automata. It discusses the implications of unambiguity in grammar and presents findings on the existence of context-free languages that cannot be generated by unambiguous grammars, highlighting the complexities and strengths of these grammatical frameworks.

The article "Semiotics and Programming Languages" by H. Zemanek explores the intersection of semiotics and programming languages, discussing how principles from structural linguistics and knowledge representation can inform programming language design and implementation. It highlights the relevance of semiotic theory in understanding syntax and semantics within computational linguistics and programming language theory.

The article discusses a novel Braille-reading machine designed to enhance the publishing and presentation of Braille text, detailing experiments on reader preferences and performance criteria for the device. It highlights the integration of natural language processing and text recognition technologies to improve accessibility for Braille users.

The article explores the cognitive processes involved in naming sequences of letters and words presented on a screen, highlighting how factors such as word type, timing, and individual letter recognition influence accuracy. It distinguishes between the mechanisms of identifying words and their order, suggesting that contextual influences can aid recognition even across longer time intervals.

The article discusses the concept of using English as a programming language, exploring its feasibility and the challenges it presents, while introducing an experimental system called DEACON that demonstrates specific capabilities in this area.

The article discusses a theoretical framework and simulation model designed to explore the semantic capabilities of a large memory system, which aims to replicate human-like language behavior by organizing and processing word meanings and their relationships. Through experiments that involve comparing and contrasting English word pairs, the model draws inferences and generates text, contributing to the understanding of knowledge representation in natural language processing.

The article examines various display-selection techniques for text manipulation in computer-aided systems, highlighting that the effectiveness of these techniques is influenced more by operational factors, such as hand control and user fatigue, rather than inherent differences in selection speed and accuracy across devices.

The article discusses the evolution of the ELIZA program, highlighting the critical role of contextual understanding in natural language conversations between humans and machines. It emphasizes that the effectiveness of communication relies on the purpose of the conversation, suggesting that achieving a high level of understanding is challenging for both parties.

The article discusses a system designed for phonemic transcription of connected speech using a microphone and analog-to-digital conversion, detailing the processes of feature extraction, classification, and analysis of utterances. It highlights successful identification of various phonemes from a single speaker's samples, achieved at a processing speed significantly faster than real-time.

The article discusses three innovative methods for extracting fundamental pitch from speech signals, including spectrum flattening with minimum phase correction, auto-correlation, and nonlinear distortion autocorrelation, highlighting their robustness against high-pass filtering and additive noise.

The article explores the intersection of linguistic methods and picture processing, focusing on how computer analysis and generation of images can be enhanced through techniques from psycholinguistics and computational linguistics, including digital pattern recognition and natural language processing.

The article discusses the automatic generation of efficient lexical processors using finite state techniques, highlighting advancements in spoken language processing and computational lexicology. It presents algorithmic developments that enhance natural language processing capabilities within computer science.

The article discusses a computer program named Zbie, designed to enhance natural language learning by utilizing structured functional descriptions and memory structures, primarily focusing on German and Russian. It emphasizes the program's ability to learn through patterns and translation rules while implementing mechanisms to minimize errors, showcasing advancements in natural language processing and computational linguistics.

The article explores how generating linking sentences enhances the recall of noun pairs in memory tasks, demonstrating that active engagement in sentence creation leads to better retention compared to passive reading. It discusses various experiments that reveal the cognitive processes involved in comprehension and memory retention, highlighting the significance of meaningful engagement in learning.

The article explores the use of the SMART automatic document retrieval system to analyze word-word associations and their impact on content analysis, highlighting how these associations can enhance both recall and precision in document retrieval. It concludes that while statistical methods improve retrieval performance, a well-constructed thesaurus outperforms these methods in achieving better results.

The article explores the dynamic interrelations between language and its underlying principles, emphasizing the work of Chomsky and his students in developing algorithms for understanding sentence structure. It discusses the interplay between language acquisition and environmental factors, drawing parallels with developmental biology to illustrate how organisms, including children, evolve through various stages of language comprehension and production.

The article presents a comprehensive analysis of 650 word-association stimuli, rated across five key characteristics: good-bad, pleasant-unpleasant, emotional-neutral, concrete-abstract, and ease of association. The findings demonstrate high reliability and alignment with existing norms, contributing valuable insights to fields such as linguistics, psycholinguistics, and natural language processing.

The article challenges the notion that natural language cannot be generated by finite state devices, proposing an alternative argument that distinguishes between iteration and recursion depth one as generative mechanisms. It asserts that these mechanisms can adequately describe complex linguistic structures, such as English embedded relative clauses and multiple-branching constructions.

The article discusses relevant models of language, drawing on insights from linguistics and natural language processing, and reflects on a paper presented at a 1969 conference focused on the intersection of language, life, and learning, with acknowledgments to Professor Basil Bernstein for his contributions.

The article "An Infinite Hierarchy of Context-Free Languages" by Sheila A. Greibach explores the theoretical framework of context-free languages, presenting an infinite hierarchy that contributes to the fields of theoretical linguistics and computer science. It discusses the implications of this hierarchy for natural language processing and computational linguistics, highlighting its significance in understanding language structure and syntax.

The article discusses the application of augmented transition network grammars in natural language analysis, highlighting their ability to perform structure-building actions that facilitate deep-structure representations and improve parsing accuracy through semantic guidance. It also includes examples and briefly outlines an experimental parsing system implementation.

The article discusses a Ph.D. thesis from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology that explores the development of structural descriptions through examples, focusing on applications in computer science, natural language processing, and machine learning. It emphasizes the intersection of deep learning and description logic in creating effective models for understanding and generating structured data.

The article presents a highly efficient context-free parsing algorithm that combines elements of Knuth's LR(k) and top-down approaches, achieving a time complexity of O(n^3) for general cases, O(n^2) for unambiguous grammars, and linear performance for a wide range of practical programming language grammars, demonstrating superior empirical results compared to existing bottom-up algorithms.

The article reviews the development of natural language question-answering systems since 1969, highlighting methods for syntactic, semantic, and logical analysis of English. It concludes that while current techniques show promise, they face challenges in generalizing to larger applications and may require innovative approaches to enhance their effectiveness.

The article discusses a study in which three children with developmental delays were trained to use past and present tense forms of verbs through imitation and reinforcement techniques. The results indicated that these methods were effective in teaching the children to generatively produce verb inflections in response to verbal prompts.

The article presents a system designed for computer understanding of English, enabling it to answer questions, execute commands, and engage in normal dialogue by utilizing semantic context and a combination of syntactic analysis and heuristic understanding to disambiguate sentences and interpret meaning within discourse.

This article reviews the impact of finite word length on the accuracy of digital filters, highlighting various techniques for analyzing this issue and presenting a comparison of theoretical and experimental results. It also discusses the calculation of statistical mean-squared error at the output, providing insights into the challenges and approaches in the field of digital signal processing.

The article discusses a thesis focused on generating semantic descriptions from drawings of scenes that include shadows, exploring techniques in image analysis, natural language processing, and computer vision to enhance scene understanding and interpretation. It highlights the integration of various fields such as machine learning and data science to improve semantic representation and evaluation.

The article explores the hypothesis that hermeneutics, traditionally associated with the interpretation of written texts, can also be applied to social sciences by treating social actions as texts that require similar interpretative methodologies. It examines the implications of this perspective for understanding meaningful action within cultural contexts and the broader field of text interpretation.

The article discusses the development of the Lunar Sciences Natural Language Information System (LUNAR), a prototype designed to enhance man-machine communication by enabling computers to understand and process natural English, thereby facilitating access to diverse scientific data and resources in lunar geology. It highlights the potential for researchers to interact with remote facilities without needing extensive knowledge of different programming languages or data formats.

The article discusses the foundational concepts and advancements in natural language processing (NLP) as presented at the 1st annual computer science conference in 1973, covering topics such as syntactic parsing, semantic evaluation, and the intersection of linguistics and computer science. It highlights the evolution of NLP techniques and their applications in text mining and automated reasoning.

The article "Towards a Semantic Description of English" explores the intricacies of semantic representation and analysis within the English language, focusing on aspects such as lexical semantics, narrative structure, and the implications for natural language processing and applied linguistics. It aims to provide a comprehensive framework for understanding how meaning is constructed and interpreted in English.

The article discusses the string-to-string correction problem, which involves calculating the minimum cost of edit operations required to transform one string into another. It presents an algorithm that efficiently solves this problem, with potential applications in areas such as automatic spelling correction and identifying common subsequences in strings.

The article discusses an algorithm designed to accurately determine the endpoints of isolated utterances in speech processing by utilizing measures such as zero crossing rate and energy, effectively minimizing data while performing well in various acoustic environments with a signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB or better. The algorithm has been tested across diverse recording conditions and speakers, demonstrating reliable performance.

The article explores the author's extensive research on the distinction between performative utterances and statements, ultimately proposing a more comprehensive theory of 'illocutionary forces' that impacts various philosophical issues related to language and communication.

The article presents a straightforward and efficient algorithm for locating all occurrences of multiple keywords within a text string, utilizing a state pattern matching machine for single-pass text processing. The algorithm's construction time is proportional to the total length of the keywords, significantly enhancing the speed of bibliographic search programs by a factor of 5 to 10.

The article presents an algorithm designed to accurately determine the endpoints of isolated utterances in speech processing, addressing the endpoint location problem by utilizing measures such as signal energy and zero crossing rate. The algorithm demonstrates effective performance in various acoustic environments, particularly in conditions with a signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB or better, and has been tested across diverse recording conditions and speakers.

The article explores how the restoration of disrupted words in sentence shadowing tasks relies on both semantic and syntactic context, highlighting the interactive parallel processing that occurs between different levels of language processing in psycholinguistics.

The article presents a new technique called discrimination value analysis for automatic text analysis, which ranks words based on their ability to distinguish between documents in a collection. It highlights the effectiveness of this method compared to traditional statistical approaches, demonstrating its potential for improving keyword extraction and content analysis in various fields such as computer science and linguistics.

The article discusses a collection of studies on the formal semantics of natural language, featuring contributions from philosophers, logicians, and linguists. It explores key topics such as quantification, reference, and the interplay between formal logic and natural language, aiming to stimulate further research in this dynamic field.

The article investigates automatic semantic processing in children and adults through a picture-word interference task, revealing that both groups experience similar levels of semantic interference when extracting meaning from printed words. The findings suggest that even by second grade, children demonstrate significant semantic processing capabilities.

The article discusses a new algorithm for the automatic segmentation of continuous speech into syllabic units, highlighting its effectiveness in identifying syllable boundaries with a low error rate. It also suggests that incorporating phonological rules for multisyllabic words could enhance the accuracy of syllable counts in spoken language processing.

This article surveys automatic speaker recognition techniques, covering speaker-dependent properties of speech signals, measurement selection methods, and experimental performance comparisons with human listeners, while discussing both text-dependent and text-independent recognition approaches.

"A Map of Misreading" is the second volume in Harold Bloom's series exploring his theory of revisionism, which illustrates how poetic imagery serves as both a reaction to and a safeguard against the influence of earlier poems. The work intersects with themes in cognitive science, language comprehension, and the detection of misinformation, emphasizing the complexities of reading and interpretation.

The article explores the application of fuzzy set theory to address vagueness and modifiers in natural language, challenging the notion of precise meanings in semantics. Through a series of empirical experiments, it examines how individuals interpret phrases with varying degrees of applicability, revealing insights into the cognitive processing of linguistic vagueness.

The article critiques the Interlanguage Hypothesis, highlighting the need for clearer definitions of its key characteristics and examining the permeable nature of interlanguages, which allows learners to transfer and distort grammatical properties from their native language. It advocates for more rigorous data collection to support hypotheses about interlanguage intuitions and discusses the implications for research strategies in linguistics and language processing.

The article explores how attention allocation affects visual word recognition using a dual-task paradigm, revealing that the complexity of secondary tasks influences memory access during word recognition, particularly highlighting that low-frequency words require more attentional resources than high-frequency words. This research contributes to our understanding of cognitive processes involved in language recognition and visual perception.

The article explores the occurrence of main clause phenomena within various subordinate clause types, challenging the notion that these constructions are strictly limited to main clauses. It argues that the conditions under which these phenomena arise are complex and involve an interplay of syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic factors, ultimately calling for a more nuanced analysis beyond existing frameworks.

The article explores the relationship between cerebral dominance and language representation, proposing that language processing involves both lateralization (interhemispheric specification) and localization (intrahemispheric organization). It examines how different types of aphasia are influenced by the degree of these elements at the time of brain damage, with a focus on comparisons between left-handed individuals and "anomalous" right-handed individuals from childhood.

The article explores the role of attention in reading by examining how irrelevant printed words can interfere with the naming of simple line-drawings, revealing that semantic associations can impact performance based on attentional focus. It suggests that while individuals can recognize words without direct attention, the semantic relevance of those words can either hinder or facilitate cognitive processing.

The article explores the application of Osgood's Semantic Differential Technique to analyze the differing perceptions of Chicano and Anglo high school students regarding key concepts related to traditional Mexican family structures, revealing significant cultural and gender-based differences in their affective meanings. The findings highlight the importance of the Potency dimension in understanding these variations between the two groups.

The article discusses ELI (English Language Interpreter), a natural language parsing program that generates meaning representations instead of syntactic structures, enabling high-level inferences and coherent episode formation in story understanding systems. It highlights ELI's unique approach to context-based analysis and provides examples of texts it has successfully interpreted.

The article explores how the background of individuals influences their interpretation of discourse, demonstrating that high-level schemata serve as interpretative frameworks. It highlights that subjects often perceive messages in a singular way, unaware of alternative interpretations, as evidenced by their performance on disambiguation tests related to different contexts.

This article explores the representation of focus attention in dialogue understanding, emphasizing its critical role in comprehension and communication. It develops mechanisms for incorporating global and immediate focus into natural language dialogue systems, highlighting how participants' focus shifts during task-oriented conversations and the implications for identifying referents in dialogue.

The article investigates the semantic effects in word processing through picture-word interference tasks across various age groups, revealing that distractor words from the same category as the pictures cause greater interference than unrelated or nonsense words. The findings suggest that both children and adults access a shared cognitive store for pictorial and verbal information.

This article discusses a representation framework for language understanding systems that enhances task-oriented dialogs by focusing on the dynamic relevance of objects and actions throughout the conversation. It highlights the importance of a knowledge base in efficiently retrieving concepts and items as the dialog progresses.

The article "Practical Reasoning in Natural Language" explores the fundamentals of reasoning, including its structure, evaluation, and common fallacies, while also addressing practical decision-making and the analysis of contemporary media editorials. It serves as a comprehensive guide for understanding and applying logical reasoning within the context of natural language processing and linguistics.

The article explores the concept of Dialogue-Games, which are structured patterns of communication that emerge in human dialogue, reflecting the goals of participants. It discusses how these patterns can be represented through knowledge structures that facilitate understanding and interpretation of dialogue, particularly in the context of natural language processing and metacommunication.

This article presents an optimized dynamic programming algorithm for time-normalization in spoken word recognition, introducing a new slope constraint technique to enhance word discrimination. It compares two time-normalized distance definitions and demonstrates the superiority of the proposed method through theoretical analysis and experimental validation, achieving significantly reduced error rates compared to conventional algorithms.

The article presents a model of text comprehension and production that emphasizes the dual levels of semantic structure—micro and macro—highlighting the cyclical nature of comprehension constrained by working memory. It discusses the role of macro-operators in summarizing information and the influence of schemas on comprehension goals, while also addressing the processes involved in text generation and recall.

The article examines the distinctions between WH-clefts and it-clefts in discourse, arguing against their interchangeability by highlighting how each structure conveys different assumptions about the listener's knowledge and thought processes. Through analysis of naturally-occurring discourse, it reveals that WH-clefts presuppose information the speaker believes the listener is already considering, while it-clefts operate under different assumptions about known facts.

The article discusses the development of a natural language interface designed to facilitate user access to complex, distributed data across computer networks, detailing the system architecture and the insulating components that convert user queries into database calls. It highlights practical features that enhance usability, such as spelling correction and run-time personalization, while also analyzing the system's limitations in comparison to other approaches in natural language processing.

The article discusses modifications to dynamic time warping algorithms used in discrete word recognition, addressing the limitations of initial assumptions regarding frame synchronization. It explores the impact of these modifications on performance, particularly in the presence of noise, and presents findings from experiments involving isolated words spoken by multiple speakers.

The article explores how schemata, or conceptual frameworks, influence the learning and recall of information in connected discourse, specifically through an experiment where college students read narratives about meals in different contexts. The findings suggest that while participants recalled food items better when they were linked to characters in the narratives, the context of the story did not significantly affect their ability to reproduce food-order information.

The article investigates the role of attention in character-classification tasks, utilizing Sternberg's additive-factors method to analyze how task demands affect choice reaction times and the automaticity of processing stages. Through a series of experiments, it concludes that while certain stages of character classification require attention, interactions between task load and target-set size suggest a complex relationship between cognitive processing and automaticity.

The article discusses advancements in speech recognition algorithms that model speech as a finite-state, hidden Markov process, introducing a method for automatically training stochastic versions of context-free grammars. It emphasizes the importance of accommodating syntactic ambiguity in natural language, allowing for explicit modeling of recognition errors rather than relying on additional components.

The article by Charles Goodwin explores how sentences are dynamically constructed and modified during natural conversations, emphasizing the role of non-verbal cues and the relationship between speakers. It highlights the implications of these findings for linguists and interactional analysts, suggesting that understanding sentences requires considering the context of their production.

The article explores a computational theory for understanding definite anaphora comprehension in English discourse, focusing on how speaker attention and an algorithmic approach can facilitate the interpretation of personal pronouns and noun phrases. It formalizes the process of focusing on specific aspects of discourse and outlines a mechanism for managing and encoding associations between elements to enhance comprehension.

The article compares various parametric representations of acoustic signals to evaluate their effectiveness in recognizing monosyllabic words within continuously spoken sentences, highlighting that a set of ten mel-frequency cepstrum coefficients achieved the highest recognition performance of 96.5% across two speakers. The study emphasizes the importance of retaining significant information amidst syntactic and duration variations in speech recognition systems.

The article explores the structure and linguistic properties of sign languages, emphasizing their unique characteristics as fully developed languages distinct from spoken language. It discusses the historical context of sign language education, the impact of oralism on deaf communities, and the implications for understanding language evolution and processing in both deaf and hearing populations.

The article investigates the time course of picture-word processing, highlighting how semantic and articulatory information from pictures and words are accessed differently. Through a series of experiments, it demonstrates that word categorization is more susceptible to interference from incongruent stimuli than picture naming, suggesting that access to word information occurs more rapidly than for pictures.

The article presents a linguistic approach to decision-making using fuzzy sets to address uncertainty, arguing that decisions should be expressed in natural language rather than numerical form. It focuses on single-stage multi-attribute decision problems and illustrates the techniques through an extensive example, highlighting the integration of linguistic approximation and truth qualification in decision theory.

The article presents a new algorithm for recognizing and parsing arbitrary context-free languages, highlighting its computational efficiency and practical implementations, with two versions achieving performance faster than cubic time. It also explores the connections between established algorithms, revealing significant similarities that enhance understanding in the fields of computer science and natural language processing.

The article presents a comparative study of various dynamic time-warping algorithms used for connected-word recognition, analyzing their theoretical differences and similarities. It includes experimental results demonstrating the performance of these algorithms in recognizing connected-digit sequences, highlighting the superiority of a specific algorithm over traditional two-level dynamic programming methods.

The article discusses the development of an improved endpoint detector for isolated word recognition, highlighting the challenges posed by nonstationary backgrounds and artifacts. It evaluates various detection techniques—explicit, implicit, and hybrid—and presents a new detector that achieves a rejection rate of less than 0.5 percent while closely matching hand-edited endpoints, resulting in enhanced accuracy for speech recognition systems.

The article presents a novel level building dynamic time warping algorithm designed for connected word recognition, demonstrating improved efficiency over existing methods while maintaining accuracy in aligning isolated reference patterns. It discusses the algorithm's implementation parameters and its application to digit recognition within the broader context of temporal pattern recognition and natural language processing.

The article explores advancements in speech recognition technology, focusing on the recognition of isolated and connected words through the integration of signal processing and information theory, while also discussing the application of modern linguistic theories to enhance recognition accuracy in controlled environments.

This article presents a novel method for breaking paragraphs into lines of approximately equal length by considering the entire text as a whole rather than line by line. Utilizing a system based on 'boxes', 'glue', and 'penalties', the approach employs dynamic programming to efficiently determine optimal breakpoints, resulting in high-quality typesetting while also providing a historical overview of line-breaking methods.

The article explores the challenges of natural language programming, focusing on three major obstacles: programming style differences, semantic interpretation, and the necessity of shared world knowledge for effective communication. It emphasizes the complexities involved in making computer interfaces more user-friendly through natural language processing.

The article discusses the validation of new names and combinations in taxonomy that were effectively published outside the International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology (IJSB), presenting List No. 6 of these validated entries. It highlights the importance of maintaining accurate nomenclature in the fields of linguistics, ontology versioning, and natural language processing.

This dissertation explores the hypothesis that the syntax of natural language can be parsed using a left-to-right deterministic mechanism, demonstrating how simple rules can effectively capture complex linguistic phenomena such as passives and yes/no questions within a context grammar framework. It emphasizes the challenges of syntactic recognition compared to competence models in theoretical linguistics and natural language processing.

The article discusses the achievable rates for multiple descriptions of independent identically distributed random variables, focusing on the relationship between distortion measures and the optimal rates for encoding these descriptions. It highlights the conditions under which expected distortions can be minimized while ensuring that the rates exceed the Shannon mutual information for the given estimates.

The article explores the challenges of interpreting verbal probability expressions used by experts in a forecasting organization, highlighting the variability in understanding these terms and the influence of context on their interpretation. It presents an experiment where experts translated verbal probabilities into numerical values, revealing significant discrepancies and suggesting practical solutions for clearer communication in forecasting.

Roget's Thesaurus of English Words and Phrases, first published in 1852, is a renowned and essential resource for writers and speakers, offering a thematic organization of words that enhances creative expression in English. With over 32 million copies sold, it serves as a vital tool for improving language command and composition across various forms of writing.

The article explores how native speakers of Ifaluk categorize and define emotion words based on situational contexts, contrasting this with the physiological organization of emotion terms in English. It highlights the influence of the Ifalukian cultural ethos on the interpretation of universal emotional dimensions, integrating insights from cognitive anthropology and ethnopsychology.

The article explores the encoding mechanisms involved in the perceptual recognition of words and pictures, analyzing how different primes affect the speed of naming targets. It concludes that while pictures generally allow for faster semantic activation, the processing of words is more attention-demanding, highlighting the complexities of automaticity in information processing and the representation of meaning.

The article presents Chat-80, a prototype natural language question answering system designed for efficiency and adaptability across various applications. Implemented in Prolog, it utilizes logic-based grammar to translate English queries into logical expressions, enabling quick and accurate responses, particularly in the domain of world geography.

The article explores intra-sentential code-switching in bilingual communities, examining how speakers seamlessly switch between languages within a single sentence and the underlying grammatical mechanisms that facilitate this interaction. It discusses the constraints and rules governing these switches, particularly focusing on closed class items within a formal computational framework.

The article critiques the effectiveness of readability formulas in assessing text readability, highlighting a case study that compares original texts for adult readers with their adaptations for less skilled readers. It argues that while some adaptations may conform to formulaic standards, true readability is influenced by factors such as discourse, logical structure, and the writer's intent, rather than merely following formulaic guidelines.

"Ways with Words" is a seminal study that explores the language development of children in two culturally distinct communities in the southeastern United States—Roadville, a white working-class town, and Trackton, an African-American community. The author examines the profound cultural differences in language use and literacy between these communities, raising important questions about communication and the impact of social context on language learning.

The article discusses ELIZA, a pioneering computer program developed at MIT for facilitating natural language communication between humans and machines, highlighting its operational mechanics, key technical challenges, and potential psychological implications, as well as future advancements in the field of artificial intelligence and human-computer interaction.

The article "Guardians and Actions: Linguistic Support for Robust, Distributed Programs" by Barbara Liskov and Robert Scheifler discusses the development of linguistic frameworks that enhance the reliability and robustness of distributed computing systems. It explores how these frameworks can facilitate better communication and coordination within distributed programs.

The article presents a comprehensive framework for understanding definite noun phrases within discourse, exploring their role in semantics and discourse structure in the context of natural language processing and computational linguistics. It aims to unify various approaches to analyzing these linguistic elements, contributing to the broader field of discourse analysis.

The article discusses a transformative shift in computational linguistics, highlighting the evolution from traditional machine translation and formal parsing to more nuanced approaches that incorporate the complexities of human language. It emphasizes current research efforts aimed at understanding natural language through realistic features and practical applications, as illustrated by various contributions in the book.

The article presents a novel non-numeric approach to risk analysis in computer systems using fuzzy set theory, emphasizing the integration of natural language expressions. It serves as a tutorial for implementing these concepts in linguistic risk analysis, bridging the fields of computational linguistics and machine learning.

The article "Incremental Context-Dependent Analysis for Language-Based Editors" by Thomas Reps and Tim Teitelbaum discusses a method for enhancing language-based editors through incremental analysis that takes into account the context of language use, aiming to improve the efficiency and accuracy of programming language processing. Published in the ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, it explores the implications of this approach for natural language processing and linguistics.

The article provides an overview of "The Foucault Reader," highlighting key concepts in Foucault's philosophy and including previously unpublished material, while also touching on various interdisciplinary applications such as natural language processing, digital scholarship, and sociology.

The article presents an iterative design methodology aimed at creating user-friendly natural language applications for office information systems, emphasizing the importance of user interface and experience in enhancing human-computer interaction. It discusses the principles and practices involved in developing effective natural language interfaces to improve usability and accessibility in office environments.

The article provides an overview of the 42nd annual meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL'04) held in Barcelona, highlighting the conference's scientific program, acceptance rates, presentation formats, and special awards, including the Lifetime Achievement Award and Best Paper Award. It emphasizes the event's commitment to showcasing cutting-edge research in computational linguistics and fostering community engagement through various sessions and social events.

The article discusses the challenges of computational tractability in frame-based description languages used for knowledge representation systems, highlighting how even minor changes in expressiveness can lead to significant intractability issues in inference computation. It emphasizes the importance of understanding these complexities for effective applications in areas such as diagnosis and planning within knowledge-based systems.

This article examines three types of aids to enhance text comprehension: tools for selecting information, methods for building internal connections, and external organizers. It emphasizes the importance of understanding how these aids influence cognitive processing during reading to improve comprehension skills.

This article presents a tutorial on a one-stage dynamic programming algorithm for connected word recognition, highlighting its efficiency and clarity compared to previous methods. The algorithm optimally synchronizes test patterns and simplifies the pattern matching process, making it suitable for real-time recognition of long sequences without the need for extensive heuristic rules.

This study investigates the strategic difficulties eighth-grade students face in summarizing texts, linking these challenges to deficits in skills such as identifying key elements and transforming text into concise summaries. The findings suggest that while students are generally aware of summarization demands, their ability to effectively summarize varies significantly, highlighting the need for targeted teaching interventions to improve comprehension and summarization skills.

The article explores how the location of initial eye fixation within isolated words affects naming and comparison tasks, revealing a U-shaped relationship where optimal fixation occurs between the third and fifth letters of words ranging from 5 to 11 letters long. Deviations from this optimal fixation point significantly increase naming times, highlighting the influence of lexical structure on visual processing.

The article explores the historical and methodological advancements in understanding the neural basis of language, highlighting various approaches such as neuropathological studies, electrical stimulation, and neuroradiological techniques that have contributed to our knowledge of language processing in the brain. It emphasizes the significance of these methods in correlating specific neural disturbances with language impairments, particularly in patients with aphasia.

The article provides an overview of the KL-ONE Knowledge Representation System, highlighting its development, core concepts, and ability to create complex structured descriptions for knowledge representation in AI. It also discusses the system's taxonomy and classification principles, along with an extended example related to a classifier recognition task.

"Mental Spaces: Aspects of Meaning Construction in Natural Language" explores the innovative concept that language expressions contribute to the creation of internally structured mental spaces rather than being interpreted solely through truth conditions. The book challenges traditional views of meaning by demonstrating how these mental constructions lead to multiple interpretations and ambiguities, emphasizing the need for theories of reference to account for the space-construction process.

The article explores how school children expand their vocabularies through incidental learning from written context, presenting a study that demonstrates measurable gains in vocabulary knowledge among eighth-grade students after reading expository and narrative texts. The findings suggest that a significant portion of vocabulary growth during school years may be attributed to learning from context, despite previous research lacking definitive support for this hypothesis.

The article provides an overview of the KL‐ONE knowledge representation system, highlighting its development, core concepts, and structured description capabilities. It discusses the system's philosophical foundations, taxonomy classification, and includes an example of its application in a classifier recognition task within the AI community.

The article explores the integration of quantitative and qualitative methods in evaluation studies, highlighting three perspectives on their relationship: purist, situationalist, and pragmatist. It argues for the benefits of combining these approaches to enhance understanding and interpretation of findings, supported by specific examples.

The article discusses a book focused on the machine-based generation of natural language text, emphasizing a formal analysis of discourse production principles that can be applied computationally. It highlights Kathleen McKeown's work in developing the TEXT program, which generates paragraph-length responses to questions, and includes examples of its operation.

This article presents a collection of new research papers from leading experts on natural language parsing, highlighting the interdisciplinary advancements in understanding how sentences are processed across psychology, linguistics, and artificial intelligence. It showcases various approaches, including psychological studies, linguistic theories, and computational models, demonstrating the significant contributions each field can make to the understanding of language parsing.

The article discusses a research initiative focused on developing a natural language processing system that employs a modular and strongly interactive model for language interpretation. It highlights the use of an activation network that integrates input, context, and long-term knowledge to resolve ambiguities in language, illustrating its effectiveness through examples of contextual influence on meaning and sentence processing.

The article examines the impact of auditory versus visual presentation modes on word learning and retrieval rates in a multitrial free recall test, highlighting its relevance for diagnosing neurological patients. It finds no significant differences in recall performance between the two modalities, but notes that response times for delayed recall are shorter for words presented auditorily.

The article discusses Dr. Appelt's book, which explores the challenges of generating natural language sentences to achieve specific speaker goals, contributing to artificial intelligence research by focusing on illocutionary acts and the speaker's beliefs. It introduces the KAMP system, which embodies this theory through a formal approach based on possible-worlds semantics and intensional logic.

The article explores the role of grammars in enhancing parsers, highlighting their significance in understanding human languages and their applications in fields such as natural language processing, computational linguistics, and machine learning. It discusses how grammars can improve knowledge representation and reasoning in syntactic and semantic parsing tasks.

The article by Michael Lesk discusses the process of automatic sense disambiguation in natural language processing, utilizing machine-readable dictionaries to differentiate between meanings of words, exemplified by distinguishing a "pine cone" from "ice cream." It highlights the significance of computational linguistics in enhancing understanding and interpretation of language through technology.

The article presents a revised report on the algorithmic language scheme, authored by J. Rees and W. Clinger, detailing advancements in algorithm design and its applications in computer science and natural language processing. Published in December 1986, it provides insights into the development and implications of the scheme within the field.

The article discusses the role of interestingness in discourse processing, highlighting the need to expand research beyond knowledge-triggered interest to include value-triggered interest. It advocates for a broader exploration of various genres and the concept of "informational significance" to enhance understanding of how interest influences cognitive processing in language.

The article presents a framework for understanding two types of mental representations formed during reading: the textbase representation and the situation model. It reports on two studies that examine how these representations influence problem-solving and recall in different age groups, highlighting their implications for instructional strategies aimed at enhancing learning.

The article examines studies comparing manual and automatic text-retrieval systems, concluding that there is insufficient evidence to suggest that intellectual content analysis yields superior results over automated methods. It highlights key concepts such as text mining, information extraction, and natural language processing within the context of information retrieval.

The article discusses various models for analyzing packet voice communication systems, focusing on the queueing behavior of packets from multiple independent sources using semi-Markov processes and continuous-time Markov chains. It compares numerical results from these models with discrete event simulations to determine the most effective modeling approach based on parameters such as generation rate and system capacity.

The article discusses a segmental k-means training procedure designed to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of connected word recognition systems, achieving recognition accuracies of 98-99% for variable-length digit strings in an airline reservation context.
