It highlights the model's ability to fine-tune on limited labeled data, demonstrating significant improvements in word error rates even with minimal pre-training.

The article presents Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR), a novel retrieval technique designed to enhance open-domain question answering by efficiently identifying relevant passages from large datasets, leveraging advancements in natural language processing and machine learning. It discusses the implications of DPR for various applications, including multimedia retrieval and knowledge discovery, within the fields of computer science and information retrieval.

The article introduces SpanBERT, an advanced pre-training method that enhances the representation and prediction of text spans by masking contiguous spans instead of individual tokens. This approach leads to significant improvements in various natural language processing tasks, including question answering and coreference resolution, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.

The article explores the benefits of domain-adaptive and task-adaptive pretraining for language models, demonstrating that additional pretraining on specific domains and tasks can significantly enhance performance across various classification tasks, even in low-resource settings. It also highlights the effectiveness of using simple selection strategies to augment task corpora when resources are limited.

The article discusses the 58th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL-2000), which was held in Hong Kong, marking the first time the event took place in Asia. It highlights the conference's focus on advancements in computational linguistics and multilingual information access, featuring a diverse technical program that encompasses various themes such as natural language processing, text mining, and machine translation.

The article introduces ELECTRA, a novel pre-training method for text encoders that utilizes replaced token detection instead of traditional masked language modeling, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and performance on downstream NLP tasks with significantly less computational resources. Through extensive experiments, it shows that ELECTRA's discriminative approach yields superior contextual representations compared to existing models like BERT and GPT.

The article introduces the Conformer, a novel convolution-augmented transformer model designed for automatic speech recognition (ASR), which effectively combines the strengths of convolutional neural networks and transformers. It reports significant improvements in word error rates on the LibriSpeech benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a parameter-efficient architecture.

The article discusses a novel approach to training visual models using natural language supervision, which allows for the development of state-of-the-art representations from a vast dataset of 400 million image-text pairs. This method enables zero-shot transfer to various downstream tasks, demonstrating competitive performance against fully supervised models without the need for additional labeled data.

The article presents a novel approach to semantic segmentation by reinterpreting it as a sequence-to-sequence prediction task using pure transformers, rather than the traditional fully-convolutional network architecture. The proposed model, SEgmentation TRansformer (SETR), demonstrates significant performance improvements on benchmark datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results in semantic segmentation tasks.

The article discusses the effectiveness of simple Siamese networks in unsupervised visual representation learning, demonstrating that meaningful representations can be learned without the need for negative sample pairs, large batches, or momentum encoders. It highlights the critical role of the stop-gradient operation in preventing collapsing solutions and presents the "SimSiam" method, which achieves competitive results on ImageNet downstream tasks, encouraging a reevaluation of architectural roles in representation learning.

This article surveys the impact of deep learning on natural language processing (NLP), offering an overview of key architectures and methods while summarizing recent research contributions across various linguistic and computational applications. It also discusses the current state of the field and provides recommendations for future advancements in NLP.

The article discusses the 2021 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP 2021), highlighting its hybrid format that combines virtual and in-person participation in Punta Cana, Dominican Republic. It emphasizes the challenges and opportunities of organizing such a conference, while acknowledging the contributions of the organizing team and the importance of remote participation for inclusivity.

The article presents mT5, a massively multilingual pre-trained text-to-text transformer model designed for natural language processing tasks. It explores the model's architecture, training methodology, and its effectiveness in handling multiple languages, contributing to advancements in multilingual deep learning and text mining.

The article discusses the development and evaluation of a domain-specific language model pretraining approach for biomedical natural language processing (NLP), demonstrating that training models from scratch on abundant biomedical text can yield significant improvements over general-domain models. It introduces a comprehensive benchmark, BLURB, for assessing various biomedical NLP tasks and highlights the release of pretrained models to accelerate research in the field.

This article offers a thorough review of over 150 deep learning models that have outperformed traditional methods in text classification tasks such as sentiment analysis and news categorization, while also summarizing 40 popular datasets and providing a quantitative analysis of model performance on benchmarks, along with insights into future research directions.

The article "Making Pre-trained Language Models Better Few-shot Learners" by Tianyu Gao, Adam Fisch, and Danqi Chen explores methods to enhance the few-shot learning capabilities of pre-trained language models in natural language processing, aiming to improve their performance in tasks with limited labeled data.

The article discusses a novel approach to zero-shot text-to-image generation using a transformer model that autoregressively processes text and image tokens as a single data stream, demonstrating competitive performance with existing domain-specific methods when trained on a large dataset.

The article presents Pattern-Exploiting Training (PET), a semi-supervised approach that enhances few-shot text classification and natural language inference by reformulating input examples into cloze-style phrases, allowing pretrained language models to better understand tasks and assign soft labels to large sets of unlabeled data, ultimately outperforming traditional methods in low-resource settings.

The article presents HuBERT, a self-supervised learning approach for speech representation that addresses challenges in speech processing by utilizing masked prediction of hidden units and an offline clustering step to generate aligned target labels. HuBERT demonstrates significant performance improvements over existing models, achieving notable reductions in word error rates on various speech recognition benchmarks.

The article introduces Codex, a GPT language model fine-tuned on GitHub code, and evaluates its Python code-writing capabilities, demonstrating its effectiveness in solving programming problems compared to other models. It also discusses the model's limitations and the broader implications of deploying advanced code generation technologies.

The article discusses the introduction of a whole word masking strategy for Chinese BERT and presents a new model called MacBERT, which enhances RoBERTa's performance through innovative modifications. It details extensive experiments across ten NLP tasks, demonstrating that MacBERT achieves state-of-the-art results and contributes valuable insights for future research in the field.

This article provides a comprehensive survey of prompt-based learning in natural language processing, highlighting its advantages over traditional supervised learning methods. It organizes existing research, introduces key concepts and mathematical notations, and offers resources for beginners to better understand this emerging paradigm.

The article discusses a method for enhancing language models' ability to follow user instructions by fine-tuning them with human feedback, demonstrating that a smaller model, InstructGPT, can outperform a larger model, GPT-3, in terms of user alignment, truthfulness, and reduced toxicity, despite having significantly fewer parameters. The findings suggest that incorporating human feedback is a promising approach to improving the performance of natural language processing models.

The article discusses "The World Atlas of Language Structures Online," a comprehensive linguistic resource edited by Matthew S. Dryer and Martin Haspelmath, which provides valuable data on the structural features of languages worldwide, facilitating research in areas such as syntax, discourse, and language documentation. It is available online through the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology.

The article investigates how chain-of-thought prompting enhances the reasoning capabilities of large language models, demonstrating that providing intermediate reasoning steps significantly improves performance on complex tasks such as arithmetic, commonsense reasoning, and symbolic challenges. Notably, a 540B-parameter model achieved state-of-the-art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark for math word problems using this prompting technique, outperforming even fine-tuned versions of previous models.

The article introduces Imagen, a cutting-edge text-to-image diffusion model that achieves exceptional photorealism and deep language understanding by leveraging large transformer models and pretrained text-only corpora. It reports a new state-of-the-art FID score on the COCO dataset and presents DrawBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating image generation quality against other leading models.

The article discusses the benefits of instruction finetuning for language models, highlighting how scaling the number of tasks, model size, and incorporating chain-of-thought data significantly enhance performance and generalization across various benchmarks. It presents findings that demonstrate substantial improvements in models like Flan-PaLM and Flan-T5, showcasing their effectiveness in few-shot and zero-shot settings compared to larger models.

The article discusses the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) as effective zero-shot reasoners, demonstrating that by using a simple prompting technique called Zero-shot-CoT, these models can significantly improve their performance on complex reasoning tasks without the need for extensive fine-tuning. The findings suggest that LLMs possess untapped cognitive abilities that can be harnessed through strategic prompting, leading to substantial advancements in their reasoning capabilities across various benchmarks.

This article presents a novel approach to deep audio-visual speech recognition, focusing on recognizing unconstrained natural language sentences from a talking face in wild videos, with or without accompanying audio. It compares two transformer-based models using different loss functions, evaluates the impact of noisy audio on recognition accuracy, and introduces a new dataset, LRS2-BBC, which significantly enhances performance benchmarks in the field.

The article introduces Open Pre-trained Transformers (OPT), a collection of decoder-only language models ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, designed to be shared responsibly with researchers. It highlights the models' competitive performance compared to GPT-3 while significantly reducing the carbon footprint of their development, and includes a logbook detailing the infrastructure challenges encountered during the process.

The article introduces BERTopic, a novel neural topic modeling approach that enhances traditional methods by utilizing class-based TF-IDF and pre-trained transformer-based language models to generate document embeddings and cluster them effectively, demonstrating competitive performance across various benchmarks in text mining and natural language processing.

The article explores the concept of emergent abilities in large language models, highlighting how these models can exhibit unexpected capabilities that are not present in smaller versions, suggesting that further scaling could enhance their performance in natural language processing tasks beyond predictable improvements.

The article introduces LaMDA, a family of advanced Transformer-based language models designed for dialog applications, highlighting their large scale and pre-training on extensive public dialog data. It addresses key challenges in safety and factual grounding, demonstrating how fine-tuning with annotated data and integrating external knowledge sources can enhance the models' reliability and relevance in various domains, including education and content recommendations.

The article introduces LLaMA, a series of foundation language models with parameters ranging from 7B to 65B, trained on trillions of tokens using only publicly available datasets. It highlights LLaMA-13B's superior performance over GPT-3 and positions LLaMA-65B as competitive with leading models like Chinchilla and PaLM, emphasizing its accessibility for the research community.

The article provides a comprehensive survey of hallucinations in Natural Language Generation (NLG), highlighting recent advancements in deep learning technologies and their impact on fluency and coherence. It reviews existing research on measuring and mitigating hallucinated text, organizes findings into general metrics and task-specific challenges, and aims to foster collaboration among researchers addressing this critical issue in NLG.

The article discusses the development and evaluation of MultiMedQA, a benchmark designed to assess the clinical knowledge of large language models (LLMs) through a combination of existing medical datasets and new consumer queries. It highlights the performance of the Pathways Language Model 1 (PaLM) and its variant, Flan-PaLM 2, in achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in medical question answering while also addressing the limitations of current models and proposing methods for improvement in clinical applications.

This article surveys the advancements in large language models (LLMs), highlighting their evolution from statistical to neural models, the impact of pre-training on performance, and the significance of model scaling. It reviews key techniques, findings, and future directions in the field of natural language processing, emphasizing the transformative effects of LLMs on both academia and industry.

The article introduces DreamBooth, a novel approach for fine-tuning text-to-image diffusion models that enables personalized image generation by embedding unique identifiers for specific subjects. This technique allows for the creation of photorealistic scenes with varied contexts while preserving the key features of the subjects, addressing challenges in tasks like recontextualization and text-guided view synthesis.

This article explores the implications of OpenAI's ChatGPT and similar AI models on academic publishing, examining their potential to automate the creation of research papers while addressing the ethical concerns that arise from their use in scholarly communication and research ethics.

The article discusses advancements in Visual Question Answering (VQA) systems by introducing multiscale feature extraction and fusion techniques for both images and text, aimed at enhancing the accuracy of information retrieval from visual data in response to questions. It highlights the challenges of effectively representing scene object information and text, and presents methods for optimizing feature extraction from deep neural networks and fusing textual data at various levels.

The article examines the effectiveness of ChatGPT in generating scientific abstracts by comparing them to real abstracts from high-impact medical journals, utilizing AI detection tools and blinded human reviewers. The findings reveal that while AI-generated abstracts are often identified as such, they can closely mimic real texts, raising ethical concerns about their use in scientific writing.

The article offers a comprehensive overview of ChatGPT, highlighting its capabilities in natural language processing, potential applications, and the challenges it faces. It emphasizes the system's ability to generate human-like conversations while acknowledging the limitations that need to be addressed for effective use.

This article surveys recent advancements in Natural Language Processing (NLP) driven by large pre-trained language models (PLMs) like BERT and GPT, highlighting their transformative impact on various NLP tasks through shared latent representations. It explores key PLM architectures, techniques for pre-training and fine-tuning, and discusses limitations and future research directions in the field.

The article highlights a study demonstrating that ChatGPT significantly outperforms crowd workers in text-annotation tasks, achieving higher accuracy and intercoder agreement while also being substantially more cost-effective. Using a dataset of over 6,000 samples, the findings suggest that large language models like ChatGPT can greatly enhance the efficiency of classification in natural language processing applications.

The article presents a novel approach to joint entity and relation extraction using Set Prediction Networks, which allows for the direct prediction of unordered relational triples from sentences without the constraints of sequence-based models. By employing transformers with non-autoregressive parallel decoding and a set-based loss function, the proposed method significantly improves accuracy and outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques on benchmark datasets.

The article explores the potential of large language models (LLMs) to enhance computational social science by effectively classifying and explaining social phenomena, providing best practices for their use, and evaluating their performance against traditional methods. It concludes that LLMs can serve as valuable tools for data annotation and creative generation in social science research.

The article presents a novel transformer-based dense captioning architecture, called TDC, which addresses limitations in existing methods by prioritizing informative regions and their relationships within complex visual scenes. By utilizing a region-object correlation score unit (ROCSU), TDC enhances the generation of detailed and contextually relevant captions, demonstrating superior performance over state-of-the-art techniques in extensive experiments.

The article discusses a novel approach to low-resource named entity recognition (NER) using a transfer learning scheme that leverages character-level neural conditional random fields (CRFs) to jointly predict entities in both high-resource and low-resource languages, achieving significant performance improvements over traditional methods.

The article introduces Deep Contrastive Representation Learning with Self-Distillation (DCRLS), a novel approach that enhances contrastive learning for time series data by integrating low-level semantic information and self-distillation techniques. The proposed method demonstrates improved performance in classification and clustering tasks across 36 UCR2018 datasets by effectively combining data augmentation and knowledge transfer between neural network blocks.

The 2024 Artificial Intelligence Index Report offers an extensive analysis of AI's evolving role in society, highlighting key trends in technical advancements, public perceptions, and geopolitical dynamics. With original data and new estimates on AI training costs, this comprehensive edition aims to provide policymakers, researchers, and the public with a nuanced understanding of the complex landscape of artificial intelligence.

The article presents an innovative AI-based algorithm for automated disaster monitoring through social media posts, utilizing natural language processing and sentiment analysis to extract location-oriented insights from global tweets. By analyzing over 67,000 entities in multiple languages, the system achieved high accuracy in identifying and understanding public sentiment during disaster events, providing valuable information for decision-makers and disaster response efforts.

The article provides a comprehensive survey on the explainability of large language models (LLMs), highlighting the importance of understanding their internal mechanisms to mitigate risks in applications. It introduces a taxonomy of explainability techniques, categorizing them based on training paradigms, and discusses methods for generating both local and global explanations, evaluation metrics, and the challenges and opportunities in the field of explainable AI for LLMs.

The article presents the Semantics-Guided Contrastive Network (ContrastZSD), a novel framework for zero-shot object detection that enhances traditional models by incorporating a contrastive mechanism and leveraging semantic information to improve detection accuracy for unseen categories. Through extensive experiments on popular benchmarks, the method demonstrates superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches.

The article reviews the advancements in natural language processing (NLP) within the context of smart healthcare, highlighting its applications in clinical practice, hospital management, personal care, public health, and drug development. It also discusses the role of NLP in addressing specific medical challenges, such as the COVID-19 pandemic, while identifying current limitations and future research directions.

The article presents a novel approach to unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for open-set person re-identification (re-ID) by introducing an end-to-end structured framework that incorporates online relation regularization. This method enhances the model's ability to adapt from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain by optimizing feature encoding and improving the quality of pseudo labels, ultimately achieving state-of-the-art results in multiple re-ID tasks.

The article provides a comprehensive survey of the GPT-3 family of large language models, including ChatGPT and GPT-4, highlighting their development, capabilities, and impact on natural language processing tasks. It discusses foundational concepts, recent advancements, and various applications, serving as a valuable resource for both academic and industry professionals to stay informed about the latest trends in large language models.

The article investigates the reasoning capabilities of large language models, such as GPT-3.5 and Llama 2, in addressing complex medical questions by evaluating their performance on established medical benchmarks. Through various prompting techniques, the study finds that these models can achieve notable accuracy, with GPT-3.5 reaching passing scores on multiple medical assessments.

edge.data: In 1964, article Computer Interpretation of English Text and Picture Patterns was published and referenced article Syntactic Analysis of English By Computer - A Survey that was published in 1899

The child paper builds on the parent paper by applying the syntactic analysis concepts discussed in the parent to a broader context that includes both English text and schematic pictures, suggesting a unified approach to interpretation. It extends the parent paper's ideas by proposing new techniques for analyzing and processing these information sources together.

In 1959, article The design and operation of the mechanical speech recognizer at University College London was published and referenced article Prediction and Entropy of Printed English that was published in 1951

The child paper builds on the principles discussed in the parent paper by applying its statistical methods to the design and operation of a mechanical speech recognizer. It extends the parent paper's concepts by integrating them into practical speech recognition technology, demonstrating their relevance in a new context.
In 1981, article Isolated and Connected Word Recognition--Theory and Selected Applications was published and referenced article Prediction and Entropy of Printed English that was published in 1951

The child paper builds on the concepts introduced in the parent paper by applying principles of entropy and prediction to the field of speech recognition, specifically in isolated and connected word recognition. It extends the parent paper's findings by integrating them with modern theories of formal language and semantic analysis to address more complex recognition problems.
In 1988, article Grammatical category disambiguation by statistical optimization was published and referenced article Prediction and Entropy of Printed English that was published in 1951

The child paper builds on the parent paper by utilizing statistical optimization techniques to improve grammatical category disambiguation, referencing the foundational concepts of entropy and prediction introduced in the parent paper. While the parent paper focuses on estimating language entropy, the child paper extends this work by applying similar statistical principles to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of language processing algorithms.
In 2000, article Two decades of statistical language modeling: where do we go from here? was published and referenced article Prediction and Entropy of Printed English that was published in 1951

The child paper builds on the parent paper by reviewing advancements in statistical language modeling and proposing future directions, including the integration of Bayesian approaches with linguistic theories, which extends the foundational concepts introduced in the parent paper regarding entropy and prediction in language.
In 2011, article Natural Language Processing (almost) from Scratch was published and referenced article Prediction and Entropy of Printed English that was published in 1951

The child paper builds on the foundational concepts of natural language processing introduced in the parent paper by proposing a unified neural network architecture that leverages statistical knowledge and internal representations, while also extending the application to various NLP tasks. However, it does not directly compare performance or experimental results with the parent paper, but rather develops a new approach that may implicitly rely on the principles discussed in the parent work.
In 1961, article THE RECOGNITION OF SPEECH BY MACHINE was published and referenced article Automatic Recognition of Spoken Digits that was published in 1952

The child paper, "The Recognition of Speech by Machine," builds on the foundational concepts of speech recognition introduced in the parent paper, "Automatic Recognition of Spoken Digits," by expanding the discussion to encompass broader aspects of speech processing and recognition technologies. While the parent paper focuses specifically on the recognition of spoken digits, the child paper likely explores additional dimensions of speech recognition, including various applications and methodologies.

In 1958, article Associative Clustering in the Recall of Words of Different Taxonomic Frequencies of Occurrence was published and referenced article The Occurrence of Clustering in the Recall of Randomly Arranged Words of Different Frequencies-Of-Usage that was published in 1955

The child paper builds on the parent paper by applying the concept of associative clustering to the recall of words categorized by different taxonomic frequencies, thereby extending the original findings on clustering in word recall. It utilizes the methodologies established in the parent paper while introducing new experimental conditions and analyses.
In 1963, article Quotients of Context-Free Languages was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper, "Quotients of Context-Free Languages," builds on the theoretical foundations established in the parent paper, "Three models for the description of language," by exploring specific aspects of context-free languages and their properties. It extends the discussion of linguistic structure and grammar by focusing on quotients, thereby inheriting and elaborating on the concepts introduced in the parent work.

In 1965, article Memory bounds for recognition of context-free and context-sensitive languages was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the parent paper by investigating the computational complexity of language recognition, specifically focusing on context-free and context-sensitive languages, which are related to the linguistic structures discussed in the parent paper. It refines and extends the concepts introduced in the parent paper by exploring memory bounds and hierarchical properties of language generation.
In 1966, article On Context-Free Languages was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the parent paper by investigating specific properties of context-free grammars, which are related to the linguistic structures discussed in the parent paper. It extends the concepts of grammatical structure and ambiguity presented in the parent paper, providing further insights into the nature of context-free languages and their relationship to finite automata.
In 1966, article English for the computer was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the concepts introduced in the parent paper by exploring the feasibility of using English as a programming language and presenting an experimental system called DEACON, which likely draws upon the linguistic structures and theories discussed in the parent paper. While the child paper does not explicitly compare performance or experimental results with the parent paper, it extends the discussion of language processing by applying the theoretical insights from the parent paper to a practical context.
In 1969, article The Finiteness of Natural Language was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the parent paper by proposing an alternative argument regarding the generative mechanisms of natural language, specifically addressing the limitations of finite state devices discussed in the parent paper. It extends the parent paper's exploration of linguistic structure by introducing new mechanisms, such as iteration and recursion, to describe complex language constructs.
In 1988, article A stochastic parts program and noun phrase parser for unrestricted text was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper, "A stochastic parts program and noun phrase parser for unrestricted text," builds on the theoretical foundations established in the parent paper, "Three models for the description of language," by applying its insights into linguistic structure to develop a practical parsing model for natural language processing. The child paper extends the parent paper's concepts by implementing a stochastic approach to parsing, thereby advancing the discussion on grammatical representation in computational linguistics.
In 1988, article Prolog and Natural Language Analysis was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper on Prolog and natural language analysis builds on the foundational concepts of linguistic structure discussed in the parent paper, applying logic programming to enhance understanding and implementation of natural language processing systems. It extends the theoretical insights of the parent paper by providing practical tools for computational linguistics.
In 2001, article A probabilistic earley parser as a psycholinguistic model was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the parent paper by utilizing the probabilistic Earley parser framework introduced by Stolcke to develop a psycholinguistic model that predicts cognitive load during sentence processing. It extends the concepts of language modeling discussed in the parent paper by applying them to the domain of human sentence comprehension and processing phenomena.
In 2011, article Natural Language Processing (almost) from Scratch was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the foundational concepts of linguistic structure discussed in the parent paper by proposing a unified neural network architecture for various natural language processing tasks, thereby extending the application of linguistic theories to practical machine learning models. While the parent paper focuses on theoretical grammar and language description, the child paper applies these insights to develop a versatile NLP system that learns from vast amounts of data.
In 2011, article Natural language processing: an introduction was published and referenced article Three models for the description of language that was published in 1956

The child paper provides an overview and tutorial of natural language processing (NLP) systems, which likely builds on the foundational concepts of linguistic structure discussed in the parent paper. While the child paper does not explicitly compare performance or experimental results, it extends the theoretical insights of the parent paper by applying them to modern NLP system design.

In 1975, article A theory of term importance in automatic text analysis was published and referenced article A Statistical Approach to Mechanized Encoding and Searching of Literary Information that was published in 1957

The child paper builds on the parent paper by introducing a new technique, discrimination value analysis, which enhances the statistical methods discussed in the parent paper for automatic text analysis and indexing. While the parent paper outlines a general approach to mechanized encoding and searching, the child paper specifically addresses the effectiveness of term importance in distinguishing documents, thereby extending the foundational concepts presented in the parent work.

In 1961, article Prediction of Word-Recognition Thresholds on the Basis of Stimulus-Parameters was published and referenced article A Note on the Informativeness of Parts of Words that was published in 1958

The child paper builds on the findings of the parent paper by investigating the impact of various stimulus parameters, including word frequency and classification, on word-recognition thresholds, thereby extending the understanding of factors influencing word recognition established in the parent study.

In 1981, article Isolated and Connected Word Recognition--Theory and Selected Applications was published and referenced article The design and operation of the mechanical speech recognizer at University College London that was published in 1959

The child paper builds on the concepts introduced in the parent paper by discussing the theoretical foundations of speech recognition and applying those principles to isolated and connected word recognition. It extends the parent paper's work by integrating modern theories of formal language and semantic analysis to address broader recognition challenges.
In 1960, article Sentences true in all constructive models was published and referenced article Sentences true in all constructive models that was published in 1960

The child paper directly inherits the content of the parent paper, as it contains identical text and concepts regarding sentences true in all constructive models. There is no indication of building upon or extending the parent paper's ideas.

In 1995, article A trainable document summarizer was published and referenced article The formation of abstracts by the selection of sentences. Part I. Sentence selection by men and machines that was published in 1961

The child paper, "A trainable document summarizer," builds on the foundational concepts of automatic summarization presented in the parent paper, "The formation of abstracts by the selection of sentences," by introducing a trainable approach to document summarization that likely extends the methodologies discussed in the parent work.
In 1977, article Frameworks for Comprehending Discourse was published and referenced article How to do things with words that was published in 1962

The child paper builds on the parent paper by applying concepts from applied linguistics and semantics to explore how background knowledge influences discourse comprehension. It extends the parent paper's focus on language and communication by providing empirical evidence on the interpretative frameworks that shape understanding in discourse contexts.
In 1977, article Dialogue‐Games: Metacommunication Structures for Natural Language Interaction* was published and referenced article How to do things with words that was published in 1962

The child paper, "Dialogue‐Games: Metacommunication Structures for Natural Language Interaction," builds on the foundational concepts presented in the parent paper, "How to do things with words," by exploring specific patterns of communication and their implications for natural language interaction. It extends the parent paper's discussion of language use by introducing the concept of Dialogue-Games, which captures the structured nature of dialogue in achieving communicative goals.
In 1990, article Introduction to WordNet: An On-line Lexical Database<sup>*</sup> was published and referenced article How to do things with words that was published in 1962

The child paper, "Introduction to WordNet: An On-line Lexical Database," builds on the concepts presented in the parent paper, "How to do things with words," by applying psycholinguistic theories of human memory to organize lexical information, thereby extending the discussion of lexical semantics and language processing introduced in the parent work.

In 1992, article Techniques for automatically correcting words in text was published and referenced article Retrieval of misspelled names in an airlines passenger record system that was published in 1962

The child paper builds on the parent paper by expanding the discussion of error correction techniques, specifically in the context of text processing, while referencing the foundational work on misspelled names in the parent paper. It surveys various detection techniques and research issues related to automatic text correction, indicating a progression from the specific application in the parent paper to a broader exploration of related methodologies.
